1: Psalm 78 is a maskîl (contemplative poem, or perhaps "teaching poem") of Asaph — one of David's three chief Levitical musicians (1 Chr 6:39; 15:17; 16:5; 25:1-2), to whose guild 12 psalms are attributed (Pss 50, 73-83). Asaph is called a "seer" (ḥōzeh) in 2 Chr 29:30, and Matthew 13:35 identifies the "prophet" who spoke the words of Ps 78:2 as "the prophet Asaph" (in some manuscripts). The psalm belongs to the genre of historical/didactic psalms that retell salvation history for pedagogical purposes: Pss 105, 106, 135, 136 are the closest parallels. The opening "listen, my people" (haʾăzînāh ʿammî) is the formula of the wisdom teacher summoning disciples (cf. Prov 1:8; 5:1; Deut 6:4) and of the prophetic oracle (Isa 1:2; Mic 1:2). The combined wisdom-prophetic register signals that what follows is both instruction and divine address.
2: "I will open my mouth with a parable (māšāl); I will speak of things hidden from ages past (ḥîdôt miqqedem)" — māšāl (parable, proverb, comparative saying, riddle) is the most versatile wisdom genre term in Hebrew, covering proverbs (Prov 1:1), allegories (Ezek 17:2-10), taunts (Num 23:7), and here the didactic recitation of hidden truths. Ḥîdāh (enigma, riddle, dark saying) suggests that the historical narrative contains embedded meanings not immediately visible — the "hidden things" that the psalm will decode. Matthew 13:35 cites this verse as fulfilled in Jesus's teaching in parables: "I will open my mouth in parables; I will utter things hidden since the foundation of the world" — applying the psalm's pedagogical program to Jesus's teaching ministry and identifying him with the Asaph tradition of prophetic instruction through enigmatic speech.
3: "What we have heard and come to know, what our ancestors passed down to us" — the chain of oral tradition that preserved covenant memory across generations in ancient Israel. Before widespread literacy, the transmission of historical and theological knowledge depended entirely on deliberate oral teaching within the extended family and at the sanctuary. Deut 6:7 mandates this transmission: "Impress them on your children; talk about them when you sit at home, when you walk along the road, when you lie down, and when you get up." The verb šāmaʿ (to hear, to listen attentively) is more than acoustic reception — it is the covenant act of receiving and obeying the tradition. The "we who have heard" are the present generation who are now responsible to become the transmitters.
4: "We will not conceal (lōʾ nĕkahēd) from their children. We will tell (nĕsappēr) the next generation of the LORD's praiseworthy deeds" — two explicit commitments: non-concealment and active recitation. Kāhad (to conceal, to hide, to suppress) — the same verb used for Job's refusal to conceal his sin (Job 6:10) and for secrets deliberately hidden; not-concealing is a positive obligation. Sāpar (to count, to recount, to tell, to declare) — the act of numbered recitation, an oral accounting. "Praiseworthy deeds" (tĕhillôt YHWH) — the same root as hallĕlûyāh (praise YHWH); the historical narrative functions as an extended act of praise. "Wonderful things" (niplāʾôtāyw, miracles/marvels) — the extraordinary acts of divine power that lie beyond ordinary human capacity.
5: "For he established a formal testimony (ʿēdût) in Jacob and ordained a law (tôrāh) in Israel, which he commanded our ancestors to teach their children" — the divine command to transmit is itself part of the Torah. ʿĒdût (testimony, solemn witness, the Testimony of the tabernacle — the ark and its contents, Exod 25:16, 21; 31:18) is here the authoritative covenant instruction YHWH deposited in Jacob/Israel. Tôrāh (teaching, instruction, law) — the comprehensive divine guidance for covenant life. The command is explicitly intergenerational: ancestors → children → grandchildren → generation not yet born (v6). This three-to-four generation transmission model reflects the family-based education system of ancient Israel, where knowledge passed primarily through parental instruction.
6: "So that the generation to come would know these things — even children not yet born — and when they grew up, would tell them to their own children" — the explicit multi-generational transmission chain: present adults → their children → those children's children. The Hebrew phrase "children not yet born" (bānîm yiwwālēdû) projects the covenant memory forward into an open future — the psalm is written not just for the present assembly but for grandchildren and great-grandchildren not yet in existence. This is the biblical foundation for the Jewish practice of the Passover Seder, in which the Exodus is narrated specifically for the benefit of the children present (Exod 12:26-27; 13:8-9). The memory-transmission is an act of covenant inclusion: to know and tell the story is to belong to the people.
7: "So they would place their confident hope (kîslām) in God, and would not forget (yišpĕḥû) what he has done, but would keep (yišmĕrû) his commands" — three covenant responses to the historical recitation: hope, memory, and obedience. Kesel (confidence, hope, the kidney as the seat of trust — anatomical) — the visceral, embodied hope that is placed in God rather than in military power or economic security. "Would not forget" — forgetting (šākaḥ) is the theological enemy: Deut 8:11-20 warns that prosperity leads to forgetting YHWH; the wilderness narrative of Psalm 78 is the anatomy of that forgetting. "Keep his commands" — the obedience that flows from memory. The three together constitute the covenantal life: trust → memory → obedience.
8: "And would not be like their ancestors — a stubborn (sôrēr) and rebellious (mārāh) generation that did not fix its heart on God and whose spirit was not faithful to him" — the negative exemplar the teaching is designed to prevent. Sôrēr (stubborn, recalcitrant) from sārar (to be stubborn like a recalcitrant animal); Deut 21:18-21 uses this term for the "stubborn and rebellious son" who refuses parental instruction and faces the community's judgment. Mārāh (to rebel, to be bitter, to be contrary) — used throughout the wilderness narrative for Israel's systematic opposition to divine guidance (Exod 17:2; Num 20:10, 24; Deut 9:7, 23-24). "Did not fix its heart (lōʾ-hēkîn libbô)" — the heart not properly directed, not made firm — the opposite of the "fixed heart" of Ps 57:7; 108:1.
9: "The men of Ephraim (bĕnê ʾeprayim), though armed with bows (nōšĕqê rômê-qāšeṯ), turned back when the day of battle came" — a historically puzzling reference. No single event in the OT narrates an Ephraimite armed retreat in battle. Possible candidates include: the defeat at Aphek (1 Sam 4) when the Philistines captured the ark; the failure at Ai due to Achan's sin (Josh 7, though Achan was from Judah); or a conflict in the period of the Judges. The theological point is clear regardless of the specific incident: Ephraim — the leading tribe of the north, Joseph's son — represents covenant unfaithfulness. Their military equipment ("armed with bows") made no difference when they lacked covenant faithfulness. Equipment without covenant integrity is useless.
10: "They failed to keep (lōʾ šāmĕrû) God's covenant and refused to live by (mēʾănû lālekeṯ bĕ) his instruction" — the Ephraimite retreat is explained as a covenant and Torah failure, not a military failure. The retreat from battle is an effect, not a cause: the cause was the prior failure to "keep the covenant" (šāmar bĕrît) and "walk in the Torah." The vocabulary of covenant-keeping (šāmar) and covenant-walking (hālak) is Deuteronomic: Deut 5:33; 8:6; 10:12-13; 11:22; 13:4-5. The psalm is making a historical-theological claim: military failure (v9) is downstream of covenant failure (v10). The history is being re-read through the deuteronomic lens.
11: "They forgot (wayyišpĕḥû) what he had done and the wonders he had shown them" — šākaḥ (to forget) is identified as the root failure. Deuteronomy uses this verb relentlessly in its warnings: "be careful you do not forget the LORD your God" (Deut 8:11); "do not forget all the benefits he has given you" (Deut 8:14). The wilderness generation witnessed the miracles firsthand and still "forgot" — the psalm is alerting later generations to how deep and dangerous the propensity to forget runs. "The wonders he had shown them" — the miracles of the Exodus and wilderness were not personal revelations to individuals but public, observable events — yet they were forgotten. This is the historical paradox the psalm meditates on.
12: "He performed remarkable deeds (pele) in plain sight (neged) of their ancestors — in the land of Egypt, in the region of Zoan (śôʿan)" — Ṣôʿan (Hebrew for Tanis) was a major city in the eastern Nile delta, the administrative capital of Egypt during the 21st and 22nd Dynasties and a significant center during the Ramesside period (19th-20th Dynasties, 13th-12th c. BCE). The explicit localization of the Exodus miracles at Zoan/Tanis connects the psalm's theology to a specific historical geography. Archaeological excavations at Tell el-Dab'a (ancient Avaris/Tanis) have uncovered evidence of Semitic populations in the eastern delta during the period corresponding to the Hyksos era and later, consistent with the Israelite sojourn in Goshen.
13: "He split the sea and brought them through; he made the water stand in walls (wayyaṣṣēb-mayim kĕmô-nēd)" — the sea-splitting is the Exodus's defining miracle (Exod 14:21-22; 15:8). Nēd (heap, mound, wall) — the water heaped up in walls, the most dramatic image of suspended natural forces. The Psalter's description of the sea walls is more vivid than Exodus's own narrative: "the waters were divided, and the Israelites went through on dry ground, with a wall of water on their right and on their left" (Exod 14:21-22). The crossing is retold as a military triumph: YHWH makes the sea a passageway by force, demonstrating his sovereignty over the chaos-waters. Later Second Temple texts (Wis 10:18; Sir 24:6) use this imagery as a foundation for wisdom theology.
14: "In the daytime he led them with a cloud (bĕʿānān), and all night long with a column of fire (kol-haLayylāh bĕʾôr ʾēš)" — the two wilderness guidance signs (Exod 13:21-22; Num 14:14; Deut 1:33; Neh 9:12, 19). The cloud and pillar of fire are the visible, tangible manifestation of the divine presence with the traveling community. They served practical purposes: the cloud provided shade from the intense desert sun (Ps 105:39; Isa 4:5), and the fire provided light and warmth in cold desert nights. Theologically, they represent divine self-disclosure in visible form: YHWH's glory (kābôd) was associated with cloud and fire (Exod 19:16-18; 24:17; 40:34-38; Num 9:15-23). The NT echoes: the cloud of the Transfiguration (Matt 17:5; Luke 9:34-35) and the pillar-of-fire presence at Pentecost (Acts 2:3).
15: "He split open rocks in the wilderness and gave them water to drink as from the great deep (kitmôm rabbāh)" — the water-from-rock miracle is narrated twice in the wilderness texts: Exod 17:1-7 (Rephidim) and Num 20:1-13 (Meribah/Kadesh). The psalm conflates both into a single act of divine provision. "As from the great deep (tĕhôm rabbāh)" — the primordial deep-water reservoir beneath the earth (Gen 1:2; 7:11; 8:2) is invoked to express the astonishing volume of water: YHWH draws on the world's foundational water supply to provide for his people. The rock-to-water miracle is one of the NT's most used types for Christ: 1 Cor 10:4 explicitly states "they drank from the spiritual rock that followed them, and that rock was Christ."
16: "He brought streams (palḡê-mayim) out of the rock and made the water flow down in rivers (kannĕhārôt)" — the intensification of the provision: not a trickle but streams, not a stream but rivers. Peleg (stream, channel, irrigation canal) is used for the streams in the temple vision of Ezek 47:1-12 and the river of the new Jerusalem (Rev 22:1). The rivers flowing from the rock in the wilderness become a standard image for divine provision in desert conditions: Isa 32:2 ("like streams of water in the desert"); 35:6 ("water will gush forth in the wilderness and streams in the desert"); 43:19-20. John 7:37-38 draws on this wilderness-water tradition when Jesus offers "rivers of living water" to those who believe.
17: "Yet they kept on sinning (wayyôsîpû ʿôd lāḥăṭōʾ-lô) against him, provoking (mārôṯ) the Most High in the wilderness" — the pattern of sin-amid-provision begins. The sinning is continuous and deliberate: yāsap (to add, to continue) means they persisted. Mārah (to provoke, to rebel against, to be bitter) — the same verb as v8 and the vocabulary of the wilderness rebellion cycle in Exodus and Numbers. "The Most High (ʿelyôn)" — one of the oldest divine epithets in the OT, possibly pre-Israelite (Gen 14:18-20; Deut 32:8), emphasizing divine sovereignty over all creation. Provoking "the Most High" in the wilderness — the place where divine provision was most lavish — is the paradox of Israelite failure.
18: "They deliberately tested (waynaṣṣû) God in their hearts, demanding food (lĕšāʾol-ʾōkel) to satisfy their cravings (lĕnapšām)" — nāsāh (to test, to put to the proof, to try) — the wilderness testing of YHWH is the great covenant offense (Exod 17:2, 7; Deut 6:16; Pss 78:41, 56; 95:8-9; 106:14). The testing is not intellectual doubt but willful demand: they imposed conditions on their loyalty to YHWH. "In their hearts" — the testing began as an interior act of will, not merely an outward complaint. "For their craving (nepeš)" — nepeš (soul, self, desire, appetite) — the craving of the self, the appetite-drive that overrides covenant loyalty. Jesus quotes Deut 6:16 ("do not put the LORD your God to the test") against Satan's temptation in the wilderness (Matt 4:7; Luke 4:12).
19: "They spoke against God, saying, 'Can God really lay out a meal here in the wilderness?'" — the direct speech of the rebellious is remarkable: they speak "against God (ʾel-ʾĕlōhîm)" — a grammatically adversarial phrase. The question is not sincere theological uncertainty but a demand-challenge: can God actually do this? The form of the question in Hebrew (hayûkal ʾēl) is the rhetorical challenge that expects a negative answer: "Can God possibly set a table in the wilderness?" The same challenge form appears in Ps 46:10; the rebellious do not believe their own history. The context in Numbers 11:4-6 shows the specific nature of the craving: they missed Egypt's fish, cucumbers, melons, leeks, onions, and garlic — concrete food items remembered with nostalgia even after the slavery was forgotten.
20: "Look, he hit a rock and water poured out, streams overflowed — but can he also supply bread? Can he provide meat for his whole people?" — the logic of the challenge: they acknowledge one miracle (water from rock) but doubt the next (bread and meat). This selective faith — accepting past miracles while doubting the next — is identified as the core of wilderness unbelief. The question "for his whole people (lĕʿammô)" suggests a corporate challenge: even if God could provide for one person, can he sustain an entire nation in the wilderness? The practical impossibility of feeding hundreds of thousands in the Sinai desert is the backdrop to the divine provision that follows.
21: "Therefore the LORD heard what they said and became furious (wayyitʿabbar); a fire broke out against Jacob, and his anger rose up against Israel" — divine hearing of the complaint results in divine anger. ʿĀbar (to pass over, to overflow, to be furious) in the hitpael — the anger that overflows containment, the burning wrath. "A fire broke out" — possibly literal (Num 11:1-3, where fire from YHWH consumed the edges of the camp at Taberah), connecting the psalm's narrative to the specific Pentateuchal event. The parallel "Jacob... Israel" in the verse uses the covenant name's two forms: Jacob (the patriarch's personal name, representing the people as descendants of the patriarch) and Israel (the new name given after Peniel, representing the covenant people defined by divine struggle).
22: "Because they refused to believe (lōʾ heʾĕmînû) in God and put no trust (lōʾ bāṭĕḥû) in his power to save (bîšûʿāṯô)" — the theological diagnosis of the wilderness rebellion: fundamental unbelief and lack of trust. ʾĀman (to believe, to be firm, to trust) — the root of ʾĕmet (truth, faithfulness) and ʾāmēn (so be it, truly). The failure to believe is not merely intellectual but relational — a failure of covenantal trust in the God who has just proven himself. Bāṭaḥ (to trust, to be confident, to lean on) — the physical metaphor of leaning on something for support. "His power to save (tĕšûʿāh)" — the same salvation-root as the name Joshua/Jesus. The wilderness generation's failure was, at its root, a failure to trust the God who saves.
23: "Yet he had already commanded the clouds above and opened the doors of heaven (daltê šāmayim pāṯāḥ)" — the shift from Israel's failure (vv. 17-22) to divine grace (vv. 23-31). "Commanded the clouds" — YHWH's speech precedes the provision; the clouds are agents responding to divine command. "The doors of heaven (daltôt šāmayim)" — the cosmological architecture of the ancient world included celestial chambers or granaries from which rain and grain were released through heavenly gates (Job 38:22-23 — "the storehouses of snow and hail"; Deut 28:12 — "the LORD will open for you his abundant storehouse, the heavens"). The image assumes a three-tier universe in which heaven's provisions are literally stored above and released through openings.
24: "He had rained down manna (wayyamṭēr ʿălêhem mānn) for them to eat and had given them grain from heaven (dĕgan-šāmayim)" — the manna miracle (Exod 16:1-36; Num 11:7-9; Deut 8:3). Mānn (manna) — from the Israelite exclamation "What is it?" (māh hûʾ, Exod 16:15); the name itself encodes the miraculous strangeness of the provision. "Grain of heaven" — the psalm identifies manna as heavenly grain, the celestial equivalent of earthly wheat. Deut 8:3 provides the theological interpretation Jesus quotes: "Man does not live by bread alone but by every word that comes from the mouth of God" — the manna was word-as-food. Jesus applies this to himself in John 6:31-35: "our ancestors ate the manna in the wilderness... I am the bread of life."
25: "Human beings ate the food of angels (leḥem ʾabbîrîm); he sent them abundant provisions" — ʾabbîr (mighty, strong one) can refer to mighty men, strong animals, or heavenly beings. The LXX renders ʾabbîrîm as aggelōn (angels), producing the "bread of angels" tradition that appears in Wis 16:20 ("food of angels, providing all pleasure and suited to every taste") and in Christian tradition. The psalm presents the manna not as wilderness emergency rations but as royal heavenly food — the finest sustenance of the celestial realm given to humans on earth. "Abundant provisions" (ṣēdāh lāśābāʿ) — food sent to the point of satiety, more than enough.
26: "He caused an east wind (qādîm) to blow through the sky; and by his power he drove the south wind (tēmān)" — the meteorological control that brought the quail (Num 11:31). YHWH directs the winds as obedient agents; the natural phenomena of wind patterns are in his service. The east wind (qādîm) and south wind (tēmān) are the significant winds of the Levantine coast: the east wind brings dry, hot air from the Arabian desert (Gen 41:6; Exod 10:13; Jonah 4:8), and the south wind brings warmth from the Negev and Sinai. Together they created the atmospheric conditions for quail migration over the Sinai peninsula — the natural mechanism of a supernatural provision.
27: "He rained meat on them as thick as dust (kĕʿāpār) and birds with wings (wĕʿôp kānāp) as numerous as grains of sea sand (kĕḥôl yammîm)" — the hyperbolic scale of the quail provision. The "dust" and "sea sand" comparisons are the OT's most emphatic expressions of uncountable multitude (Gen 13:16; 22:17; 32:12 — the patriarchal covenant promises used this formula for the offspring). Applying this formula to the quail emphasizes that the provision was not merely adequate but overwhelmingly abundant. The quail migration (śĕlāw) was a known seasonal phenomenon in the Sinai region: migrating quail exhausted by their Mediterranean crossing would land in great masses, making them easy to catch (Exod 16:13 records the same provision).
28: "He dropped it right in the middle of their camp (bĕqereb maḥănēhû), all around where they lived" — the provision is delivered to the people's location rather than requiring them to seek it. The placement "in the middle of the camp" emphasizes divine attentiveness and generosity: the food comes to them. The camp structure in Numbers 1-2 places the tabernacle at the center surrounded by the tribal groupings — so "the middle of the camp" is also in the vicinity of the divine dwelling. The food from heaven lands around the place where God himself dwells among his people.
29: "They ate and were completely satisfied (wayyiśbĕʿû meʾōd); he had given them exactly what they wanted (wĕtaʾăwāṯām yābîʾ lāhem)" — the satisfaction formula closes the provision. Śābaʿ (to be satisfied, to have enough, to be full) — the covenant promise of Deut 6:11; 8:10, 12 ("when you eat and are satisfied... do not forget the LORD"). Satisfaction becomes the spiritual danger: plenty leads to forgetting. "Exactly what they wanted (taʾăwāh)" — the desire/craving of vv. 18 and 30 is satisfied, but satisfaction does not produce gratitude — it produces the next phase of rebellion (vv. 30-31).
30: "But they had not turned from their craving (lōʾ-zārû miṯtaʾăwāṯām); the food was still in their mouths (bĕpîhem ʾōkelām)" — the craving persists even while eating. The desire-machine of the self (nepeš) is not satisfied by satisfaction — it merely shifts to the next craving. Numbers 11:33 records the chronology: "while the meat was still between their teeth and before it could be consumed, the anger of the LORD burned against the people." The food in the mouth and the continued craving together represent the insatiability of desire disconnected from gratitude. Ecclesiastes 6:7 makes the same observation: "All man's effort is for his mouth, yet his appetite (nepeš) is never satisfied."
31: "When God's wrath came down on them and slew the strongest among them (bĕmišmannêhem) and struck down the young men of Israel (ûbāḥûrê yiśrāʾēl) hikkîaʿ" — the divine judgment falls precisely on the vigorous and healthy — the "fattest" and the "young men." Mišmān (fatness, the fattest) — the irony: those who craved meat until they were corpulent died of divine judgment while still eating. The death of the young men (bāḥûrîm) — the warriors, the future of the community — is the most devastating social loss in a tribal society where young male fighters represent the group's survival capacity. The Numbers account locates this at Kibroth-hattaavah ("graves of craving," Num 11:34) — the place named for this episode of divine judgment.
32: "Despite all of this (bĕkol-zōʾt), they went on sinning and refused to believe (lōʾ-heʾĕmînû) even his miracles" — the refrain of the great historical psalms: "despite all of this" (also Ps 106:7, 13, 21, 43). The witness of miracles did not produce faith; punishment did not produce repentance; provision did not produce gratitude. This is the psalm's central theological claim: human sinfulness runs deeper than experience, evidence, or consequence. The Exodus generation had witnessed more divine acts per capita than any generation before or since, and still "refused to believe in his miracles." This paradox drives the psalm's eventual turn to the David-Zion solution (vv. 68-72).
33: "So he made their days dissolve into emptiness (hayyêhem bāhebel) and their years end in sudden fright (ûšĕnôṯām babbĕhālāh)" — the covenant consequence: the wilderness generation's lives became hebel (breath, emptiness, vapor — the Ecclesiastes word) — insubstantial, without permanent accomplishment. Bĕhālāh (terror, sudden fright, dismay) — the fearfulness of lives spent in anxiety and judgment rather than covenant security. This is the inverse of the covenant promise of Deut 28:1-14 (long life, fruitfulness, security) — the wilderness generation experienced the life-diminishment of covenant failure. Numbers 14:26-35 records the divine decree: no adult member of the Exodus generation except Caleb and Joshua would enter the promised land.
34: "Whenever he struck them down (im-hăragām), they would search for him (dĕrāšûhû); they would turn around and sincerely seek God (šiḥărû-ʾēl)" — the pattern of adversity-provoked repentance. Only under divine punishment did the people "seek" (dāraš) and "sincerely seek" (šāḥar — to seek early/diligently, as one rises early to search) for God. Šāḥar is the intensive seeking at dawn; the same word describes Wisdom's seekers in Prov 8:17. But this seeking is crisis-driven rather than covenant-love-driven — it is the repentance of pain, not the repentance of gratitude or devotion. Verses 36-37 will expose the shallowness of this repentance.
35: "They recalled (wayyizkĕrû) that God was their rock (ṣûrām) and the Most High God was their redeemer (gōʾălām)" — the memory that suffering provokes. Ṣûr (rock, cliff) as a divine title emphasizes immovability, refuge, and the reliable solidity of YHWH as distinct from human fragility. Gāʾal (to redeem, to act as kinsman-redeemer, to buy back) — the kinship-redemption institution in which the nearest male relative had the obligation to buy back sold land, ransomed persons, or to marry a dead kinsman's widow (Lev 25:25-28, 47-55; Ruth 2:20; 3:9-13). God as kinsman-redeemer (gōʾēl) applies the kinship-loyalty institution to the divine-human covenant relationship: YHWH acts as the elder kinsman who buys back his enslaved family members.
36: "But they only said what he wanted to hear (waypattûhû bĕpîhem); they spoke to him with lying tongues (ûbilšônām yĕkazĕbû-lô)" — the repentance was verbal performance, not authentic transformation. Pātāh (to persuade, to entice, to flatter, to be open — in the piel: to flatter, to deceive by flattery) — the words of v. 34-35 ("they recalled, they sought") turn out to be flattery without sincerity. "Lying tongues (lāšôn kāzāb)" — the tongue that says what the other wants to hear without intending to follow through. Isaiah 29:13 (quoted by Jesus in Matt 15:8; Mark 7:6) makes the same charge against Israel: "These people honor me with their lips, but their hearts are far from me" — the psalm's v. 36 is the historical prototype of the prophetic charge.
37: "Their hearts were not truly directed toward him (lōʾ-nākôn libbām ʿimmô); they were not faithful to his covenant (wĕlōʾ neʾĕmĕnû bĕbĕrîṯô)" — the inner reality behind the verbal performance. Kûn (to be established, to be firm, to be directed) — the heart that is nākôn is steady, constant, reliably oriented. The wilderness generation's heart was unstable, turning with circumstances. "Were not faithful (neʾĕmān)" to the covenant — the same ʾāman root as "believe" (v. 22): their belief was as faithless as their verbal repentance. The theology of authentic covenantal relationship requires both inner orientation (fixed heart) and behavioral consistency (covenant faithfulness) — the wilderness generation lacked both.
38: "Yet he, being full of compassion (raḥûm), covered over (yĕkappēr) their guilt and did not destroy them; again and again he turned back (yāšîb) his anger and did not unleash all his fury (ḥămāṯô)" — the most theologically dense verse of the psalm: divine compassion overriding deserved judgment. Raḥûm (compassionate, merciful) — from reḥem (womb); the divine compassion is maternal-womb tenderness (Exod 34:6 names this first in the divine self-description). Kāpar (to cover, to atone, to wipe away) — the cultic term for atonement (the kappōret on the ark; Yom Kippur). Divine compassion performed its own atonement in the wilderness, covering guilt without requiring the full payment of judgment. "Again and again he turned back his anger" — the divine restraint is continuous and repeated; YHWH's mercy is as persistent as Israel's rebellion.
39: "He remembered that they were only flesh (kî-bāśār hēmmāh) — a passing wind (rûaḥ hôlēk) that does not come back" — divine compassion is grounded in divine realism: YHWH acts with full knowledge of human fragility. Bāśār (flesh) — the vulnerable, mortal material of human existence, subject to disease, weakness, and death (Isa 40:6: "all flesh is grass"). "A passing wind (rûaḥ hôlēk) that does not come back" — the brevity of human life expressed in the wind metaphor (Pss 39:5, 11; 90:5-6; 103:15-16). The logic of divine compassion: because humans are flesh-and-wind — mortal, fragile, and brief — YHWH adjusts his response to their capacity. This is not a lowering of moral standards but a parental awareness: "as a father has compassion on his children, so the LORD has compassion on those who fear him; for he knows how we are formed, he remembers that we are dust" (Ps 103:13-14).
40: "How many times (kammāh) they defied him in the wilderness and caused him grief (yaʿăṣîbûhû) in the wasteland!" — the grief of God is a theological affirmation: YHWH is not impassible (unaffected by events) but genuinely grieved by Israel's rebellion. ʿĀṣab (to grieve, to cause pain, to wound emotionally) — the same verb used for God's grief at human wickedness before the flood (Gen 6:6: "the LORD was grieved that he had made human beings on the earth"). Eph 4:30 ("do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God") takes this language directly into NT theology. The "how many times?" is the anguished rhetorical count of a parent who has absorbed repeated disappointment without destroying the relationship.
41: "They kept turning back (wayyāšûbû) and testing God (waynaṣṭû-ʾēl), pressing against (wehitweû) the Holy One of Israel (qĕdôš yiśrāʾēl)" — the cycle resumes: tested (v18), provided for (vv. 23-31), judged (vv. 31-34), repented shallowly (vv. 34-37), forgiven (v38), and now testing again. "Pressing against" (tāwāh — to mark, to press, to wound) the Holy One of Israel — the pressure-testing of the divine: they push against YHWH's patience as against a boundary. "The Holy One of Israel" (qĕdôš yiśrāʾēl) is Isaiah's characteristic divine title (Isa 1:4; 5:19, 24; 10:20; 12:6; 17:7; 29:19; 30:11-12, 15; 31:1; 37:23; 40:25 — 25 times in Isaiah). The psalm's use of this Isaianic title signals the prophetic tradition's diagnosis of the same failure.
42: "They forgot (lōʾ zāpĕrû) what his hand had done, and the day (yôm) he rescued them from their oppressor" — the key theological term "the day" (yôm) — the Day of the LORD's rescue is the paradigm event (Exod 12-15) that should define Israel's entire subsequent history. Forgetting "the day" of rescue is forgetting one's own identity and origin. Deuteronomy returns obsessively to this day: "remember that you were slaves in Egypt and the LORD your God brought you out of there" (Deut 5:15; 15:15; 16:3; 24:22; 25:17; 32:7). The history that follows in vv. 43-51 is the specific content of what they forgot — the catalog of plagues and miracles becomes an accusation against the forgetting community.
43: "How he had displayed his miraculous signs (ʾōṯōṯāyw) in Egypt and his wonders (môpĕṯāyw) in the region of Zoan" — ʾôṯ (sign) and môpēṯ (wonder, portent, prodigy) are the standard paired terms for the Exodus plagues (Exod 7:3; Deut 4:34; 6:22; 7:19; 11:3; 26:8; 29:3; Jer 32:20-21; Neh 9:10). The pair appears 11 times in Deuteronomy alone as the foundational category for divine self-revelation. "Signs and wonders" in the NT: Jesus performs "signs" (sēmeia) in John's Gospel (John 20:30-31); the Apostolic healings are "wonders and signs" (Acts 2:43; 4:30; 5:12). The Exodus sign-and-wonder framework becomes the interpretive grid for recognizing divine action in history.
44: "He turned their rivers into blood (dāmîm) so that they were unable to drink from their streams" — the first plague (Exod 7:14-24). The Nile was the lifeblood of Egypt — its annual inundation deposited the rich silt that made the Nile valley the most productive agricultural land in the ancient world. To turn the Nile to blood was to strike at Egypt's fundamental survival mechanism. The Egyptian deities Khnum (guardian of the Nile's source), Hapi (the Nile flood personified), and Osiris (associated with the Nile's rejuvenating water) were implicitly challenged by this act: YHWH controlled what Egypt's gods supposedly governed.
45: "He sent swarms of flies (ʿārōb) to devour them, and frogs (ṣĕpardĕʿîm) that brought ruin upon them" — the fourth plague (flies, Exod 8:20-32) and the second plague (frogs, Exod 8:1-15) are mentioned in reverse order. ʿĀrōb (swarms of flies, or possibly biting insects/horseflies) — the exact species is uncertain; the LXX renders it as kynomyia (dog-flies/horseflies). The plague of frogs attacked Egypt at its most sacred level: the frog-goddess Heqet was an Egyptian deity of fertility and childbirth associated with resurrection. The plague filled every household with the creature Egyptians venerated — a theological challenge as well as a practical plague.
46: "He handed their harvest over to the locust (ḥāsîl) and their labor to the swarming caterpillar (ʾarbeh)" — the eighth plague (locusts, Exod 10:1-20). ʾArbeh (locusts) — the locust swarm was the agricultural catastrophe of the ancient Near East; Joel 1-2 describes a locust invasion as apocalyptic in scale. The destruction of harvest represented the complete negation of the agricultural year's labor — months of work reduced to nothing in hours. Egypt's agricultural prosperity (sustained by the Nile) is specifically targeted: what the flood created, the locusts consumed.
47: "He struck their vines down with hail (bārad) and their sycamore trees with killing frost (ḥănāmāl)" — the seventh plague (hail, Exod 9:13-35). Vines were precious agricultural wealth (Num 13:23-24; Deut 8:8) — Egypt's vineyards produced both food and wine for both domestic and export use. Sycamore figs (šiqmāh) were a staple food of the poor in Egypt and Canaan; their destruction added food insecurity to agricultural devastation. The specification of the killed crops (ḥānāmāl — possibly "cutting frost" or "freezing rain") suggests the psalmist drew on detailed tradition about the specific agricultural effects of this plague.
48: "He surrendered their cattle (bĕhemtām) to the hail and their flocks (miqnêhem) to lightning strikes (lĕrĕšāpîm)" — the fifth plague targeted livestock (Exod 9:1-7); here the destruction is attributed to hail-lightning (the seventh plague's thunderstorm). Rešep (lightning, burning coal, plague) is the name of a Canaanite deity of plague and lightning; the word here demythologizes the plague-deity into an impersonal divine instrument. By using the Canaanite plague-deity's name for YHWH's hailstorm, the psalm implicitly claims that what the Canaanites attributed to Reshep, YHWH actually commands.
49: "He unleashed his fierce anger upon them — his wrath (ʿebrāṯô), indignation (zaʿam), and distress (ṣārāh) — a company of destroying messengers (malʾăkê rāʿîm)" — the cluster of divine anger-words: ʿebrāh (overflow-anger, raging fury), zaʿam (indignation, divine anger at injustice), ṣārāh (trouble, distress, strait — here the distress YHWH releases on Egypt). "Destroying messengers (malʾăkê rāʿîm)" — the destroying angels of the tenth plague (Exod 12:23: "the destroyer"). These are heavenly agents executing divine judgment — not independent evil forces but messengers sent on YHWH's command. Heb 1:14 draws on this ANE messenger-army tradition: angels are "ministering spirits sent to serve those who will inherit salvation."
50: "He made a clear way (yĕpallēs ʾôraḥ) for his wrath; he did not withhold their lives from death, but surrendered them to the plague (laddeber)" — pālas (to level, to weigh, to make smooth) — the construction metaphor of building a road: YHWH leveled the path for his wrath to travel without obstacle. The verb used for road-building in the wilderness (Isa 40:3: "prepare the way for the LORD, make straight in the desert a highway for our God") is here used for the path of divine judgment. Deber (plague, pestilence) — one of the covenant curse categories in Lev 26:25 and Deut 28:21; Rev 6:8 lists "death, famine, plague, and wild beasts" as the four horsemen's instruments.
51: "He killed all the firstborn in Egypt (bĕḥōr bĕʾereṣ-miṣrāyim), the prime of their strength (rēʾšît ʾônîm) in the dwellings of Ham (bĕʾōhŏlê-ḥām)" — the tenth plague, the death of the firstborn (Exod 11:1-12:30). "Prime of their strength (rēʾšît ʾônîm)" — the firstborn as the embodiment of the father's generative power and family future (Gen 49:3: "Reuben, you are my firstborn, my might, the first sign of my strength"). To kill the firstborn was to extinguish the future of every Egyptian family. "The dwellings of Ham" — Egypt is a "son of Ham" in the table of nations (Gen 10:6; 1 Chr 1:8; Ps 105:23, 27; 106:22); the genealogical identification ties the Exodus narrative into the primeval history of Genesis.
52: "But he brought his own people out like sheep (kaṣṣōʾn) and led them through the wilderness like a flock (ʿēder)" — the pivot from judgment to salvation: what was done against Egypt (vv. 43-51) is reversed for Israel (vv. 52-55). The shepherd metaphor for divine leadership (Ps 23; Isa 40:11; Ezek 34; John 10; Heb 13:20; 1 Pet 2:25; Rev 7:17) is here traced to the Exodus: the shepherding began when God led Israel through the wilderness. Ṣōʾn (flock, sheep and goats) and ʿēder (herd, flock) — the pastoral vocabulary of the leading shepherd who guides, protects, and provides for dependent animals.
53: "He guided them in safety (wayyanḥēm lābĕṭaḥ) so that they had nothing to fear; the sea swallowed their enemies (kissāh hayāmmāh ʾôyĕbêhem)" — the safety of the guiding shepherd expressed in contrast to the fate of the enemies: Israel walks in security while Pharaoh's army is swallowed by the sea (Exod 14:26-31; 15:4-5, 10). Nāḥāh (to lead, to guide, to conduct) is the shepherd's leading of the flock through terrain the sheep cannot navigate alone. The sea that was an obstacle for Israel (v13: he split it) becomes an executioner for Egypt: divine power employs the same natural force in opposite ways for the two parties.
54: "He brought them to his sacred territory (ʾereṣ qodšô), to the mountain that his right hand had won (har-zeh qānĕṯāh yĕmînô)" — the goal of the Exodus journey: "his sacred territory" (ʾereṣ qodšô) — the land of Canaan as divine possession and gift (Exod 15:17; Lev 25:23: "the land is mine"). "The mountain that his right hand had won" — possibly Mount Zion specifically (Ps 2:6; 48:1-2) or the hill country of Canaan generally. Qānāh (to acquire, to possess, to create/beget — as in Gen 14:19, 22: "God Most High, Creator of heaven and earth") — the land is YHWH's by right of creation and by act of conquest: both creator and warrior have title to it.
55: "He expelled the nations (wayĕgāreš gôyim) from before them, assigned their land as an inheritance (naḥălāh) by lot, and made the tribes of Israel to dwell in their territory" — the Conquest as divine military action (Josh 3-12; Deut 11:23; Ps 44:2-3). "By lot (bĕḥebel)" — the inheritance division by measuring-line/lot (Josh 18:1-10; Mic 2:5); the lot system appealed to divine decision: the territory assignment was YHWH's choice, not merely military success. The tribe-by-territory assignment in Joshua 13-21 is the implementation of this divine lottery. The expelled nations were the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites (Deut 7:1) — the six-to-seven nation list that frames the Conquest theology.
56: "Nevertheless they tested (waynaṣṢû) and defied the Most High God and refused to keep his commandments (ʿēdôṯāyw)" — the cycle repeats at a new stage: after the Exodus provision (vv. 13-31), after the Conquest settlement (vv. 52-55), the same pattern emerges. Each new act of divine grace is followed by a new cycle of testing and rebellion. "Refused to keep his commandments (ʿēdôṯ, testimonies/decrees)" — the covenant stipulations of the Torah. The failure to keep the commandments in the land is the violation that Deuteronomy warns will result in exile (Deut 28:15-68) — which eventually happens (though Ps 78 ends before the exile, with the David-Zion resolution).
57: "They turned back (wayyissōgû) and acted faithlessly (wayibgĕdû), just as their ancestors had; they bent away (nehehpĕkû) like a defective bow (qešeṯ rĕmiyyāh)" — three images for covenant unfaithfulness: turning back (the retreat of Ephraim in v9 recurs), betrayal (bāgad, to act treacherously, to deal faithlessly), and the defective bow. Qešeṯ rĕmiyyāh (slack bow, unreliable bow) — a bow that misfires, whose string is slack when tension is needed; the warrior cannot depend on it at the critical moment. The bow-metaphor appears in Hosea's condemnation of Ephraim (Hos 7:16: "they are like a faulty bow"). The defective weapon is more dangerous than no weapon — it creates false confidence.
58: "They made him angry (yakkîsûhû) with their hilltop shrines (bāmôṯêhem) and roused his jealousy (qinʾāṭô) with their carved idols (pĕsîlêhem)" — the specific covenant violation that provoked divine abandonment: the high places (bāmôṯ) and idols. Bāmāh (high place, elevated shrine) — the hilltop worship sites scattered through Canaan, used for both legitimate (1 Sam 9:12-14; 1 Kgs 3:4) and illegitimate (1 Kgs 12:31-32; 14:23; 2 Kgs 17:9-11) worship. The Deuteronomic reform of Josiah (2 Kgs 23) was specifically aimed at destroying the bāmôṯ. Divine "jealousy" (qinʾāh) — the covenant term for YHWH's exclusive claim on Israel's loyalty; the Second Commandment names YHWH as "a jealous God" (Exod 20:5; Deut 4:24; 5:9; 6:15).
59: "When God heard all this, he was incensed (wayḵeʿas); he thoroughly rejected (wayyimʾas meʾōd) Israel" — the divine response to the high-place idolatry. Kāʿas (to be provoked, to be incensed, to become angry) — particularly the anger provoked by the specific offense of covenant unfaithfulness (also 1 Sam 1:6-7; 1 Kgs 15:30; 16:2, 13, 26). Māʾas (to reject, to despise, to refuse) — the most severe term for divine covenant refusal; used for the rejection of Saul (1 Sam 15:23, 26; 16:1) and of the northern kingdom (2 Kgs 17:20). "Thoroughly (meʾōd) rejected" — the intensifier meʾōd (very, greatly, exceedingly) makes the rejection complete and unqualified.
60: "He abandoned (wayyiṭṭōš) the tabernacle at Shiloh (miškan šilô), the tent he had established among his people (bāʾādām)" — the abandonment of Shiloh is one of the OT's most traumatic historical events. Shiloh was the central sanctuary of the tribal confederacy (Josh 18:1; 19:51; 21:2; 22:9; Judg 21:19; 1 Sam 1:3), where the tabernacle rested for several generations. The battle of Aphek (1 Sam 4:1-11) resulted in the Philistines capturing the ark — and Shiloh was apparently destroyed shortly afterward. Archaeological excavations at Khirbet Seilun (Shiloh) conducted by Y. Shiloh (1981-1984) found evidence of destruction in the 12th century BCE, consistent with this narrative. Jeremiah repeatedly cited Shiloh's destruction as a warning to Jerusalem: "Go now to the place in Shiloh where I first made a dwelling for my Name... Look at what I did to it because of the wickedness of my people Israel" (Jer 7:12; 26:6).
61: "He allowed his strength (ʿûzzô) to go into exile (šĕbî) and his glory (tipʾartô) into enemy hands" — the ark is described as YHWH's "strength" and "glory" — the visible symbol of divine presence and power. In 1 Sam 4:21-22, when Phinehas's wife hears of the ark's capture, she names her newborn son "Ichabod" (ʾî kābôd): "the glory has departed from Israel, because the ark of God was captured." The loss of the ark was experienced as the loss of the divine presence itself — YHWH allowing his own symbolic dwelling to fall into Philistine hands. The use of "exile" (šĕbî) for the ark's removal anticipates the later vocabulary of Babylonian exile.
62: "He surrendered (wayyasgēr) his people to the sword and turned against his inheritance in anger" — divine abandonment becomes active opposition: YHWH does not merely withdraw protection but actively delivers Israel to the sword. Sāgar (to shut, to close, to deliver up — as to a creditor or an enemy) — the delivering-up of someone into the power of their enemy. "His inheritance" (naḥălāṯô) — Israel as YHWH's own portion and inheritance (Deut 9:29; 32:9; Ps 33:12; Jer 10:16) — what YHWH turns against is his own treasured possession. The theological scandal: the divine warrior who conquered Canaan for Israel now fights against Israel.
63: "Fire devoured their young men (baḥûrāyw ʾākĕlāh ʾēš), and their young women received no marriage praise (lōʾ hullālû)" — the social consequences of military defeat on the next generation. The young men (bāḥûrîm) killed in battle = the marriage generation eliminated. "Young women received no marriage praise (hālaṭ)" — the marriage celebration songs (hālal, from the same root as hallelujah) that were not sung because there were no young men left to marry (cf. Jer 7:34; 16:9; 25:10; Rev 18:23 — "the voice of bride and groom will never be heard in you again" as the ultimate sign of desolation). The absence of wedding song is the absence of future.
64: "Their priests (kōhănêhem) were cut down by the sword; their widows could not even weep (lōʾ tibkênāh)" — the death of Hophni and Phinehas (1 Sam 4:11, 17-18) and the collapse of the Elide priesthood. The widows who cannot weep: possibly because the deaths were so sudden that there was no time for mourning; or possibly because the catastrophe was so overwhelming that normal mourning customs broke down. In ANE culture, women specifically performed the mourning rituals — the professional mourning women appear in Jer 9:17-18; Ezek 32:16. Widows who cannot mourn their dead husbands represent the complete social breakdown that accompanies military catastrophe.
65: "Then the Lord awoke (wayyîqaṣ) as if from sleep (kĕyāšēn), like a warrior who rises vigorous from wine (miyyayin)" — the most anthropomorphic image in the psalm: YHWH sleeps, then wakes energized. The sleeping deity is a common motif in ANE literature: Baal's annual sleep during winter in Ugaritic mythology (cf. Elijah's mockery of Baal in 1 Kgs 18:27: "Perhaps he is sleeping and must be awakened"). Psalm 78 subverts this motif: while the nations' gods truly sleep (because they are not real), YHWH's "sleep" is temporal non-intervention, not actual incapacity. "Vigorous from wine (gibbôr mĕʿôn-yāyin)" — a warrior empowered and energized, his faculties at peak alertness. The comparison is boldly anthropomorphic and serves to emphasize the decisiveness and power of the divine intervention that followed.
66: "He drove his enemies back (wayyak ṣārāyw ʾāḥôr) and put them to shame that will not end (ḥerpāh ʿôlām)" — the decisive divine counter-strike against the Philistines after the period of ark-captivity (1 Sam 5-7). "Shame that will not end (ḥerpāh ʿôlām)" — the permanent, eschatological disgrace; contrasted with Israel's temporary shaming by defeat. In 1 Sam 5:6-12, YHWH afflicted the Philistines with tumors (ʿŏpālîm) wherever the ark traveled among their cities, forcing its return. The Philistine defeat is not recorded in Psalm 78 in detail but is implied by the shift to the David-Zion conclusion.
67: "He refused (wayyimʾas) the tent of Joseph and did not choose (lōʾ-bāḥar) the tribe of Ephraim" — the theological conclusion of the northern rejection: YHWH did not choose Joseph/Ephraim as the tribe through whom the covenant would be administered. The rejection of Ephraim reverses the blessing of Joseph's sons in Gen 48, where Jacob specifically blessed Ephraim over Manasseh and declared that Ephraim's "younger brother will be greater than he." The psalm does not explain why Ephraim was rejected; it simply states the divine sovereign choice. The rejection of Shiloh (vv. 60-64) = the rejection of Ephraim; both represent the northern rejection that paved the way for Judah's election.
68: "Instead he chose (wayyibḥar) the tribe of Judah — Mount Zion, which he loved (ʾāhēb)" — the sovereign divine election is stated in the most direct terms: "bāḥar" (to choose, to select, to elect). The election of Judah over Ephraim (Joseph) is the surprising reversal of human primogeniture: as Jacob reversed it (Gen 48), so YHWH reversed the tribal expectations. "Mount Zion, which he loved" — divine love (ʾāhab) for Zion is a distinctive theme: Pss 87:2; 132:13-14 ("the LORD has chosen Zion, he has desired it for his dwelling"). Zion's election is permanent and rooted in divine affection, not merely strategic advantage.
69: "He built his sanctuary (miqdāšô) like the heights of heaven (kĕmô-rāmîm), like the earth that he has set in place forever (ʾereṣ yĕsādāh lĕʿôlām)" — the temple in Jerusalem as the cosmic mountain, the point where heaven and earth meet. Miqdāš (sanctuary, holy place) — the term for the sacred enclosure of the tabernacle/temple (Exod 25:8; Lev 26:2; Num 18:1). "Like the heights of heaven" — the ANE cosmic mountain theology: the divine dwelling is simultaneously on earth (as a geographical location) and in heaven (as the cosmic center). "Like the earth he set in place forever" — the temple's permanence parallels creation's permanence; as the earth endures, so (by this promise) the temple endures. The destruction of the temple in 586 BCE made this psalm's promise a theological crisis that the prophets (Jeremiah, Ezekiel, Isaiah 40-66) had to address.
70: "He chose David (wayyibḥar bĕdāwîd) his servant (ʿabdô) and took him from the sheep pens (mikmiklĕʾōṯ)" — the Davidic election is stated in identical language to the Zion election (v68: wayyibḥar). David and Zion are co-elected, inseparable components of the covenant structure that the psalm builds toward. "His servant (ʿebed)" — the ʿebed YHWH (servant of YHWH) designation is one of the highest honors in the Psalter; Moses is "the servant of the LORD" (Deut 34:5; Josh 1:1), and the Isaianic Servant in Isa 40-55 is the eschatological fulfillment of this title. "Sheep pens (miḵlāʾ)" — the enclosures where sheep were kept at night. The pastoral origin of David is the basis for the shepherd-king typology (Ezek 34:23-24; John 10:11; Rev 7:17).
71: "From tending the nursing ewes (mēʾaḥar ʿāllôṯ) he brought him to shepherd (lirʿôṯ) Jacob his people, and Israel his inheritance" — the literal shepherding of nursing ewes becomes the typological preparation for the metaphorical shepherding of Israel. Nursing ewes require the most careful handling — patience, gentleness, and skill — because they must care for their young while meeting their own needs. The care required for nursing flocks is the same care required for the covenant people. The move from literal (ʿāllôṯ, nursing ewes) to metaphorical (ʿammô, his people / naḥălāṯô, his inheritance) is the psalm's climactic typological statement.
72: "He tended them with the integrity of his heart (bĕtōm lĕbābô) and guided them with the skill of his hands (ûbĕtĕbûnaṯ kappāyw)" — the closing characterization of the ideal Davidic shepherd-king: integrity (tōm, wholeness, completeness, blamelessness) of heart and skillful (tĕbûnāh, discernment, understanding, practical wisdom) guidance. Both qualities are required: the right motivation (integrity) and the right capacity (skill). Tōm is the Job-term for blamelessness (Job 1:1, 8) and the psalmist's own moral integrity (Pss 7:8; 25:21; 26:1, 11). Tĕbûnāh (understanding, insight, the practical wisdom of the craftsman) appears frequently in Proverbs as the second register of wisdom alongside ḥokmāh. The psalm closes not with a call to action but with a portrait of the ideal king — the answer to the covenant crisis, the solution to the wilderness failure.
